speaker: interviewer
text: Let's pick up from when you were running away from the massacre at the farm and following two men, and then they, they lost you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were alone again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was alone. And that night I spent in a wooded area. And I remember not even eating or anything all day long. I did-- I do remember, that I, I, I dug up potato-- a raw potato I was eating that night. The reason I remember because it happened to be a rotten potato. with a horrible taste. And, you know, that's-- The next day I start heading back toward that area, toward that-- not particularly there. But I was familiar with that part of the, the villages, and so I was heading and walked all day long. And then I stopped at a farmer's place. And, and they gave me food, lunch. And he said, you know, he says, there was about 10 people, I think, survived that, that whole-- that, you know, killing. And I think once they survived, he says, they're not going to kill them. It's like, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I listened to him. And since not knowing where to go anyway, I was heading-- I thought I'll go there. And that was true. There was about 10 people survived that, and they came back there. They had no place to go, and I guess at least there was a place there. It was-- So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came there. And I remember this young couple. It was a brother and sister lying. It was getting cold, I guess, already. Yeah, it was some cold days. And they were-- I don't know whether they were frost bitten somehow. But they had a-- I smelled decay in their legs. I don't remember exactly what the reason was. Were they alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were living. Yeah, they were living there. I mean, they were lying. They couldn't walk. They couldn't get up-- very sick. And she was a very beautiful woman. She must be-- must have been 20 or 21. And he also was a young man. I don't know what happened to them, how they got sick, but I do remember that. And that's the first time in my life I watched two people die. She died first, and then he died. And there were eight others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay with them while they were dying?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed. Well, I don't know what happened. I think the others buried them the next day. I don't remember exactly how it came about, but I was-- I was there. We watched them. We watched them die. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after that, we were there for a few days. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one night, two uniformed policemen came. It was getting dark. It was dark already, or getting dark. And they had us all come o-- all, all eight people come out and line up on a wall. And they were going to kill us. One guy was right on the corner of that house. He took off. He started running. And once policeman chased him and starts firing after him. And while-- and the other guy, the other policeman was with a rifle pointing to us. I begged him to let me go. And I guess he felt sorry for me, and he says, run little one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was running the same direction with the other guy was running. And I teamed up with this man, who-- I was yelling after a while. You know, we were both running the same direction, and it was plowed fields. And, and he stopped, and we started walking together. We walked all night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And early in the morning, we came to a, a village. I don't remember the town, but we just came into the first house. And it happens to be a Czech people. And Czech people, they felt sorry for us, and they fed us. They gave us some food, and we had a nice breakfast. And we got out. While we got out, we started walking. It was-- the, the road was muddy, I remember. And he walked on one side of the road. I walked on the other side heading the same direction. I saw somebody start chasing us, some guy chasing us by foot. And he was mostly loo-- chasing my-- this friend, whoever he was. I don't remember his name at this time. And, and looking at me like, for you, he didn't-- with me, he wouldn't have a problem, but he's, he's mainly, you know, the bigger guy. He knows he could get me any time. This guy was running fast. He was running so fast that faster than I was. I was-- I think I had a, a warm coat on or something and uncomfortable shoes that were not comfortable or oversized or something. It wasn't right. I just couldn't. And it was muddy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while he was running after him-- and then there was quite a distance between he and, and this guy was running. The guy was very fast. I start running back because this way I thought I have a better chance back to the village. We were just almost on the outskirts of the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was running like crazy. Well, he gave up on him, and he start chasing after me. He was already a distance from me. So this man ran into a barn. It was just a lot of these places were open. And he grabbed a horse, and he was chasing me with a horse. And I ran towards the first farm I've seen. It was like a, a fence, a sm-- not very high fence. And there was-- it's a huge farm with a lot of straw. This was freshly-- well, what am I tr-- the-- the-- there were a lot of fresh stacks of straw, that it's early in the fall, and it's harvest season. So I ran towards the far-- to that-- I got to that fence, and he was almost right there. And I jumped over the fence, rolled over. And I made a dive in the straw. There was lots of straw, fresh straw. And, and, and by the time he got off the horse, and he started climbing over that little fence, he came towards the area. I could hear his foots-- feet on the straw. And he was starting looking and looking and going further away. And, and, and he just couldn't-- I'm sure he couldn't understand what happened to me. But I, I made-- you know, he just couldn't see me. And then dogs were barking. Soon a stranger comes, dogs always bark. And people from that farmer-- I mean from that farm didn't know what the hell was going on. I hear-- I hear them talk. It wasn't very clear, but I heard them. And they, they don't know what's going on and they were looking and looking and looking. And this went on till lunchtime. Oh, the reason I was-- I knew it was lunchtime because then they start-- I don't know I used to know by what they would do, how they feed the, the chickens or whatever. And then things got quiet. That means he was gone. The dogs quit barking, so I knew that he was gone. And when everything got quiet, in, you know-- I stuck my head out. And there was just nothing but lots of straw. Some of it's maybe a foot high, two feet, and then these huge stacks of straw. So I got close to one of those stacks of straw and got closer to it. And I camouflaged myself real well. I thought and I'm going to-- you know. And it's like I'll just stay there, see what happened. During the day I had no place. I didn't know what to do because I was afraid to be in the open field. Well after lunch, things were all over again. This guy is back. I hear him talk to these people. And the dogs are barking. And then they're looking for me. And, you know, and this went on for another-- I don't know-- a couple hours, and then quiet again. And I had no idea whether it was dark or not because I didn't move. I was hidden, just didn't move. I finally, thinking, you know, when they fed-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's-- it just-- I don't remember exactly, the chicken or something they would call. And, and I felt that it's getting dark or it's getting late, later. And I thought I'll just maybe get out of there. I stuck my head out, and there was still some little daylight, not much. It was getting darker. And there was a young woman came to get some straw with a pitchfork. And I stuck my head out just about the same time she started getting straw, and she saw me. I scared her to death. And she screamed out, and she, you know, was running back to the house. Then I thought, boy, that's-- so I had to go. I had to run. So I start running in back of the buildings and, and cultivated black field. Once I got on the field, I was-- you know, it was open field, and that was just running and running. And there was-- there was-- there was a time that I, I could have been dead if things didn't work out that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this still the fall of 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, yes, the fall of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your physical condition at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe your physical condition at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My physical condition, I, I was-- at that time I was 13, 12, 13-years-old, I guess. '42--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to bathe at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? No. No, I didn't do that. Didn't have enough clothes. I mean, I, I-- no, I didn't. The only time I washed myself was during the summer. But during the winter, I just didn't do anything. I wasn't even-- I didn't even stay in a home. I slept in barns and, and just in straw and hay where I could sneak in, and people didn't know that I was even there. That, that night, I remember, when I was running all night on the field, I came to another farmer. And I tied to sneak into the barn, just to get in, because the doors were not locked. People didn't have any locks. But once I got in there, the dog started barking. I mean, you know, he barked and barked and barked. And god, I hoped that he would quit. And the farmer came out to see what's going on. I mean, he knew that something isn't right. The dog was barking. But I was hidden in the straw. And I guess the dog after a while got tired of barking, and that was it. And I spend all night there and without food. And I think I ate that morning, the morning before. The next day I was walking again, wandering. And it just-- the reason I did that, because I had no place to be. I had to move. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Later that winter, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in a different area, and I was-- oh, I remember one time. There was a terrible storm, a winter storm. And I was walking. I couldn't even see the road. But you always spot a house, so you head for the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this 1943, now? Or early--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. This is 1943. You know, I never knew days or weeks or months, I mean, but that's-- yeah, it was winter of '43. And I came to this house. It was sort of a dilapidated poor farmhouse. And I remember there was an-- the woman, she looked older, but she had kids. And the husband worked in the flour mill. She told me that. And she let me stay at the house. I mean, it was blowing, you know, like a, a winter storm. And I stayed there for several days. And they were extremely poor people. They all-- all they had is flour that he would bring from the mill. And she would make things almost-- I mean she would bake things. I don't know what she used. I mean, there was like no sugar and no salt or anything like that. It was just pastry, tasteless, but, you know, whatever it was, it was OK. And I stayed there for several days. She was just a nice person, a good person. And she felt, felt sorry for me. Most people felt sorry when they saw me, but they just wouldn't do anything. Well I stayed with her for maybe a week. The weather got different, and I left. And I was-- remember crossing from one village to another. I-- that, that was on a Saturday night. That, I remember. It was getting slightly dark, darker. And I was walking with maybe two feet of snow on the road. You can hardly see. There was some a tr-- a sled. At that time, they would drive-- use sleds instead of wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the, the hat that I wore, one of those kind of rounded thing. It's a fur, fur type cap. It's a winter hat. And I had a little coat that I carried. It had bullet, bullet holes. When I was escaping from that place, I had that coat. I, I had it in my-- I carried that. It was holes in it from-- I figured out later there was-- they were firing from the place where we escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had-- but the shoes, I don't remember exactly. I had some-- at that time, I still had some decent shoes on. The reason I remember that because there was a, a farmer. I came into the farm, and the woman wanted my shoes. I said, what am I going to wear? She wanted my shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think I ran away from him. I was afraid of her. So I guess my shoes were still decent at that time. But meanwhile, while I'm walking from that village, and, and I can see a house, oh I don't know, maybe if I would estimate, less than a quarter of a mile. I would see-- you know, they were lighting lamps. They used to use kerosene lamps. It was lit up. I could see it from a distance. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was getting darker. I saw a man walking towards me. And that always scared the hell out of me. I knew that-- you know, because I-- you never know. A man-- it's sort of strange to have a man doing that. Normally you don't see anybody walking in that kind of weather. I mean, I'm walking. I have no choice. But this guy walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he came very close to me, well my, my heart was, was pounding, was just beating. And he says to me, where are you going? I said, I'm going home. He says, where's your home? And I named a village. At that time, I was familiar with every place. He says, where have you been? I said, I've been at my uncle's. And he looked at me. It's like at first, he, he got me, you know? He knew there was something. But by asking these questions-- and this is one time that I felt like God put words in my mouth because I was-- I was fluid. I was quick, my responses to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, OK, little guy. He says, you going home, go home. And when I passed him, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I wanted to fly. I mean, I wanted to run, but I was afraid to do anything. You know, I'm going one way. He was going the other way. And after a while I start running. And the first house that I saw where they were lighting-- you know, that was lit up, I ran into that house. And, and she says, you lucky. She says, Medynski just left the house. That's the guy. He would have killed me. He was just like a, a Jew hunter. I mean that's what he was. He was a head hunter. He would get paid for killing a Jew. I heard about the guy. I never knew him. I never knew that. I heard in villages. People, you know, occasionally farmers would say things to me. You know, there were nice people. You know, you've got to watch. You've got so and so looking for you. He knows there was a kid. He doesn't-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and that's one day. She says, he was in my house. He was leaving. Well, they didn't look out the window to see what's going on. They couldn't see it. But when I told them that, they knew that that's the same I came in contact with. What village was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's like you know when you go out and you go by, you see a few houses. There was just this-- I don't remember the name. I lost-- it's amazing that some of the villages I remembered for so long, and now I don't remember them. But just small places, they're very small, scattered houses with one road. And this went on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Same thing, I was doing the same thing, wandering and walking and, and hiding, until I came to-- this was already in the spring that I remember I came to a Czech village. And I don't remember that name now. I think it-- and, and somebody said-- no. I came to this-- he was a head of the village, a Czech family. And he asked me if I wanted to work for him. He says he doesn't want to hire any Jews. I said, well I'm not a Jew. He said, that's good. It's like he knew, but he wanted to make it clear that he didn't hire a Jew. No one is a Jew. But I remember that. I said, yeah, I'm not Jewish. He says, OK. Then I was taking care of-- he had a couple cows. He had-- there was a bull and two horses. It's a small-- he was a small farmer. He was also busy with some-- you know, I don't know how much-- as a head of the village, how busy he. It was really not a great deal, but he-- but he didn't have as much time to work on a farm as others did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there for a long time. I mean, I was there all spring and summer. And meanwhile, at the same time, there was another Jewish kid in that village about my age also doing the same thing, working for another farmer. So we got to know each other. And at night we would spend some time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until one time, the farmer comes and he says, you know, he says, the, the Ukrainians got your friend, or this kid. He didn't say my friend, but he said that kid that was working for this farmer. So when I heard that, I, I ran away, because I knew that they-- they're going to come after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that, there was another instance while I was working for this farmer. There were two Ukrainians came in with rifles. And they wanted-- and they-- I was-- I was the only one on the outside. You know, when they drove in, I was right near the barn. And they asked me, he said, where is your father? And, and there were-- just the way they asked that because I was not dressed as of one of the kids, you know? I mean, my clothes-- I was not dressed that great. But they asked me, so I answer that in Czech, that he's not there, but he'll be back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they turned around and left. Well, I was scared to death. I thought, you know, if they would have known that I was Jewish, I'm sure they would've gotten me and shot me. And I think right after that, when I heard about my friend, that they, they got him out, and they shot him. And I was afraid that when before they do that, they'll torture him because they want to know about the Jews, of other people who are there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's when I, I-- well, I was sc-- I took off for good. I didn't come back to that farmer. You were afraid that they were going to torture him to get information about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Exactly. And, and, you know, and I couldn't have ever blame them for that because I know what they would do. And I don't know how I would have acted. [SIGHS] Before that-- I just want to tell you how-- I mean, all of these instances that it comes to mind. I wasn't-- when I was working for him during that summer, there was a-- I would take the cows out on the field and, and I was watching the cows. There was a Jewish man that I knew was hiding in that village. I used to bring him food because he was scared. And I was able to do that, to sneak out some food. And, and during the summer, he had a place in the field, an open field. It was-- I mean, it was not open, but it was like corn or wheat that there was hidden. Nobody could see him. And he didn't-- you know, he was not in a-- in a barn or anything. And I would bring him food as often as I could. He was-- I don't know-- probably in his 40s at that time. I thought he was an older guy. And one time, I knew where he was. And it wasn't that far from where I would take care of the cows. And there was a bunch of police-- Ukrainian police-- walking through the open field. And they were looking for people. They knew there was somebody hiding. There's always somebody that would squeal that they knew there was a Jew somewhere in the field, but they didn't know exactly where.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they came by and they ask me. He says, you know of any Jews? I says, no, I haven't seen any. And they were walking this f-- there were five of them. And they had rifles. I remember they carried that by the straps, and the rifle was sort hitting-- hidden. It's and the field were-- was-- I don't know what it was. Was it like a wheat field or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I watched them pick up that man. They found him. I mean, found him. He was right there as they went by him. And, and they followed him. He was walking in front of them, and all these five had guns. And they were walking into the direction. There was a forest. And you could see because it was-- I don't know how far. It could have been maybe a half a mile, but you could see. And it was slightly elevated, and I could see him all the way until they got into the wooded area. And like, I don't know, minutes later-- I don't know exactly the amount of time-- I heard a shot was fired. And I don't know what I did at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop the tape right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a-- yeah.
